[[ja:城輪神社]] {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kinowa Shrine | image = [[File:Kinowa-jinja Haiden.JPG|250px|Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall]]
Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall | location = Kinowa, Omotomononimi 35, [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|38|57|59.00|N|139|54|36.50|E|scale:10000_region:JP-06_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinowa Shrine}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] | shrine_rank = [[Shakaku|Nationally recognized]]; [[Dewa Province]]'s [[Ichinomiya#Ninomiya|Ninomiya]]
[[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]] | founded = Unknown | honden = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festivals = August 17 (festival) | rituals = Mikashira-shinji (Sacred Head Ceremony) | map = {{location map|Japan Yamagata|lat_deg=38|lat_min=57|lat_sec=59.00|lon_deg=139|lon_min=54|lon_sec=36.50|label=Kinowa Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} [[File:Kinowa-jinja Gate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Torii Gate]] [[File:Kinowa-jinja Honden.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Main Hall]] [[File:Chokai with Kinowa-jinja.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine grounds. View from the northwest corner of the Kinowa Historical Park, located at the site of the ancient Kinowa Palisade. The mountain in the background is [[Mount Chōkai]].]] '''Kinowa Shrine''' (城輪神社, ''Kinowa-jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan. It was discovered in [[1931]] that the shrine is situated at the site of an ancient castle palisade, confirming its role as the guardian deity of the fortification. It is the [[Ninomiya]] of the [[Dewa Province]] and formerly held the rank of [[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]]. Although it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'', it appears in the ''[[Six National Histories|Rikkokushi]]'' as a [[Shakaku|Nationally Recognized Shrine]]. == History == The exact date of founding is unclear, but according to *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*Abe Masami, *Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho, Vol. 5: Jōrin no Dewasaku-shi Oyobi Kokubun-ji-shi Chōsa* (National Publishing Co., November 1982)., it is presumed that the shrine was established sometime between the construction of {{ill|Jōrin Castle|ja|城輪柵}} in the first year of the Wadō era (708) and the second year of the Reiki era (716). According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, a historical record written by the shrine's priest in the 4th year of the An'ei era (1775), the shrine was enshrined during the reign of Emperor Keikō. However, *Ihitsu no Suji*, written in Ansei 6 (1859), dismisses this claim as doubtful. In May of Shōwa 6 (1931), the ruins of Jōrin Castle were discovered, revealing that the shrine was located between the north gate and northeast corner of the castle's northern perimeter. The *Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple* states that the enshrinement along the northern perimeter gave the deity its name, Jōrin no Kami.*Ihitsu no Suji* (1859) proposes an alternate theory, suggesting that the name "Jōrin" derives from combining the kanji for "Jō" from Yamato Province's {{ill|Jōjō District|ja|城上郡}} and "Rin" from {{ill|Mount Miwa|ja|三輪山}}. It further hypothesizes that this deity was the tutelary protector of the Dewasaku, established to pray for the pacification and subjugation of the Emishi in the northeast, a region fortified heavily due to frequent attacks. Place names near the shrine, such as Miyagata, Tamada, Terayashiki, and Kamiyuda, became more meaningful after the discovery of Jōrin Castle ruins. Particularly, *Ninomiya Kokonki* notes that Miyagata referred to lands cultivated by shrine farmers, while Kōdengata referred to fields owned by the provincial government. According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, the enshrined deity of Jōrin Daimyōjin is Kuraokami-no-Mikoto, said to be identical to the deity of {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}. However, this claim is dismissed as speculative in *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*. While absent from the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* (927), the *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, dated Jōgan 7 (865), records that the "Jōrin deity of Dewa Province" was promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In Gangyō 4 (880), the deity was further elevated to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade alongside deities such as {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, {{ill|Gassan Shrine|ja|月山神}}, and {{ill|Komonoimi Shrine|ja|小物忌神社}}.Edited by Tanigawa Kenichi, *The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 12: Tohoku & Hokkaido* (Hakusuisha, June 1984). The *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku* mentions Jōrin Shrine primarily after the 9th century, reflecting its prominence following the full establishment of Jōrin Castle. However, the shrine is absent from records after the medieval period, likely due to its decline during the Sengoku era. During the Edo period, the shrine was revitalized under the Sakai clan, lords of the Shōnai domain. In An'ei 4 (1775), a new shrine building was constructed. Later records indicate further repairs and contributions during the Hōei and Tenpō eras, as well as the rebuilding of the shrine after a fire in Meiji 6 (1873).Yamagata Prefecture Shrine Office, *Yamagata Prefecture Shrine History* (2000). In the modern era, the shrine underwent several reconstructions and administrative changes. It was designated as a Prefectural Shrine in Shōwa 7 (1932) and continues to host sacred rituals and cultural events, such as the *Mikomai* dances performed by local children. == deity == * [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] (倉稲魂命) == Auxiliary Shrines == * Tamaike Shrine * Kōtaijin Shrine == Access == * [[East Japan Railway Company|JR]] [[Uetsu Main Line]] [[Sakata Station]]: 20-minute bus ride * JR Uetsu Main Line [[Mototate Station]]: 30-minute walk == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Abe, Masami. ''Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho Vol. 5: Kinowa no Dewa Saku Ato Oyobi Kokubunji Ato Chōsa''. Kokusho Kankōkai, 1982. * Tanikawa, Kenichi (ed.). ''Nihon no Kamigami - Jinja to Seichi Vol. 12: Tōhoku/Hokkaidō''. Hakusuisha, 1984. * Yamagata Shrine Bureau. ''Yamagata Ken Jinja-shi''. revision.text:
[[ja:城輪神社]] {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kinowa Shrine | image = [[File:Kinowa-jinja Haiden.JPG|250px|Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall]]
Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall | location = Kinowa, Omotomononimi 35, [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|38|57|59.00|N|139|54|36.50|E|scale:10000_region:JP-06_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinowa Shrine}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] | shrine_rank = [[Shakaku|Nationally recognized]]; [[Dewa Province]]'s [[Ichinomiya#Ninomiya|Ninomiya]]
[[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]] | founded = Unknown | honden = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festivals = August 17 (festival) | rituals = Mikashira-shinji (Sacred Head Ceremony) | map = {{location map|Japan Yamagata|lat_deg=38|lat_min=57|lat_sec=59.00|lon_deg=139|lon_min=54|lon_sec=36.50|label=Kinowa Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} [[File:Kinowa-jinja Gate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Torii Gate]] [[File:Kinowa-jinja Honden.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Main Hall]] [[File:Chokai with Kinowa-jinja.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine grounds. View from the northwest corner of the Kinowa Historical Park, located at the site of the ancient Kinowa Palisade. The mountain in the background is [[Mount Chōkai]].]] '''Kinowa Shrine''' (城輪神社, ''Kinowa-jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan. It was discovered in [[1931]] that the shrine is situated at the site of an ancient castle palisade, confirming its role as the guardian deity of the fortification. It is the [[Ninomiya]] of the [[Dewa Province]] and formerly held the rank of [[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]]. Although it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'', it appears in the ''[[Six National Histories|Rikkokushi]]'' as a [[Shakaku|Nationally Recognized Shrine]]. == History == The exact date of founding is unclear, but according to *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*Abe Masami, *Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho, Vol. 5: Jōrin no Dewasaku-shi Oyobi Kokubun-ji-shi Chōsa* (National Publishing Co., November 1982)., it is presumed that the shrine was established sometime between the construction of {{ill|Jōrin Castle|ja|城輪柵}} in the first year of the Wadō era (708) and the second year of the Reiki era (716). According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, a historical record written by the shrine's priest in the 4th year of the An'ei era (1775), the shrine was enshrined during the reign of Emperor Keikō. However, *Ihitsu no Suji*, written in Ansei 6 (1859), dismisses this claim as doubtful. In May of Shōwa 6 (1931), the ruins of Jōrin Castle were discovered, revealing that the shrine was located between the north gate and northeast corner of the castle's northern perimeter. The *Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple* states that the enshrinement along the northern perimeter gave the deity its name, Jōrin no Kami.*Ihitsu no Suji* (1859) proposes an alternate theory, suggesting that the name "Jōrin" derives from combining the kanji for "Jō" from Yamato Province's {{ill|Jōjō District|ja|城上郡}} and "Rin" from {{ill|Mount Miwa|ja|三輪山}}. It further hypothesizes that this deity was the tutelary protector of the Dewasaku, established to pray for the pacification and subjugation of the Emishi in the northeast, a region fortified heavily due to frequent attacks. Place names near the shrine, such as Miyagata, Tamada, Terayashiki, and Kamiyuda, became more meaningful after the discovery of Jōrin Castle ruins. Particularly, *Ninomiya Kokonki* notes that Miyagata referred to lands cultivated by shrine farmers, while Kōdengata referred to fields owned by the provincial government. According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, the enshrined deity of Jōrin Daimyōjin is Kuraokami-no-Mikoto, said to be identical to the deity of {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}. However, this claim is dismissed as speculative in *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*. While absent from the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* (927), the *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, dated Jōgan 7 (865), records that the "Jōrin deity of Dewa Province" was promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In Gangyō 4 (880), the deity was further elevated to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade alongside deities such as {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, {{ill|Gassan Shrine|ja|月山神}}, and {{ill|Komonoimi Shrine|ja|小物忌神社}}.Edited by Tanigawa Kenichi, *The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 12: Tohoku & Hokkaido* (Hakusuisha, June 1984). The *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku* mentions Jōrin Shrine primarily after the 9th century, reflecting its prominence following the full establishment of Jōrin Castle. However, the shrine is absent from records after the medieval period, likely due to its decline during the Sengoku era. During the Edo period, the shrine was revitalized under the Sakai clan, lords of the Shōnai domain. In An'ei 4 (1775), a new shrine building was constructed. Later records indicate further repairs and contributions during the Hōei and Tenpō eras, as well as the rebuilding of the shrine after a fire in Meiji 6 (1873).Yamagata Prefecture Shrine Office, *Yamagata Prefecture Shrine History* (2000). In the modern era, the shrine underwent several reconstructions and administrative changes. It was designated as a Prefectural Shrine in Shōwa 7 (1932) and continues to host sacred rituals and cultural events, such as the *Mikomai* dances performed by local children. == deity == * [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] (倉稲魂命) == Auxiliary Shrines == * Tamaike Shrine * Kōtaijin Shrine == Access == * [[East Japan Railway Company|JR]] [[Uetsu Main Line]] [[Sakata Station]]: 20-minute bus ride * JR Uetsu Main Line [[Mototate Station]]: 30-minute walk == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Abe, Masami. ''Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho Vol. 5: Kinowa no Dewa Saku Ato Oyobi Kokubunji Ato Chōsa''. Kokusho Kankōkai, 1982. * Tanikawa, Kenichi (ed.). ''Nihon no Kamigami - Jinja to Seichi Vol. 12: Tōhoku/Hokkaidō''. Hakusuisha, 1984. * Yamagata Shrine Bureau. ''Yamagata Ken Jinja-shi''. Yamagata Shrine Bureau, 2000. {{commonscat|Kinowa-jinja}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kinowa Shrine}} [[Category:Kinowa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall | location = Kinowa, Omotomononimi 35, [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|38|57|59.00|N|139|54|36.50|E|scale:10000_region:JP-06_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinowa Shrine}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] | shrine_rank = [[Shakaku|Nationally recognized]]; [[Dewa Province]]'s [[Ichinomiya#Ninomiya|Ninomiya]]
[[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]] | founded = Unknown | honden = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festivals = August 17 (festival) | rituals = Mikashira-shinji (Sacred Head Ceremony) | map = {{location map|Japan Yamagata|lat_deg=38|lat_min=57|lat_sec=59.00|lon_deg=139|lon_min=54|lon_sec=36.50|label=Kinowa Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} [[File:Kinowa-jinja Gate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Torii Gate]] [[File:Kinowa-jinja Honden.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Main Hall]] [[File:Chokai with Kinowa-jinja.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine grounds. View from the northwest corner of the Kinowa Historical Park, located at the site of the ancient Kinowa Palisade. The mountain in the background is [[Mount Chōkai]].]] '''Kinowa Shrine''' (城輪神社, ''Kinowa-jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan. It was discovered in [[1931]] that the shrine is situated at the site of an ancient castle palisade, confirming its role as the guardian deity of the fortification. It is the [[Ninomiya]] of the [[Dewa Province]] and formerly held the rank of [[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]]. Although it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'', it appears in the ''[[Six National Histories|Rikkokushi]]'' as a [[Shakaku|Nationally Recognized Shrine]]. == History == The exact date of founding is unclear, but according to *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*Abe Masami, *Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho, Vol. 5: Jōrin no Dewasaku-shi Oyobi Kokubun-ji-shi Chōsa* (National Publishing Co., November 1982)., it is presumed that the shrine was established sometime between the construction of {{ill|Jōrin Castle|ja|城輪柵}} in the first year of the Wadō era (708) and the second year of the Reiki era (716). According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, a historical record written by the shrine's priest in the 4th year of the An'ei era (1775), the shrine was enshrined during the reign of Emperor Keikō. However, *Ihitsu no Suji*, written in Ansei 6 (1859), dismisses this claim as doubtful. In May of Shōwa 6 (1931), the ruins of Jōrin Castle were discovered, revealing that the shrine was located between the north gate and northeast corner of the castle's northern perimeter. The *Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple* states that the enshrinement along the northern perimeter gave the deity its name, Jōrin no Kami.*Ihitsu no Suji* (1859) proposes an alternate theory, suggesting that the name "Jōrin" derives from combining the kanji for "Jō" from Yamato Province's {{ill|Jōjō District|ja|城上郡}} and "Rin" from {{ill|Mount Miwa|ja|三輪山}}. It further hypothesizes that this deity was the tutelary protector of the Dewasaku, established to pray for the pacification and subjugation of the Emishi in the northeast, a region fortified heavily due to frequent attacks. Place names near the shrine, such as Miyagata, Tamada, Terayashiki, and Kamiyuda, became more meaningful after the discovery of Jōrin Castle ruins. Particularly, *Ninomiya Kokonki* notes that Miyagata referred to lands cultivated by shrine farmers, while Kōdengata referred to fields owned by the provincial government. According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, the enshrined deity of Jōrin Daimyōjin is Kuraokami-no-Mikoto, said to be identical to the deity of {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}. However, this claim is dismissed as speculative in *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*. While absent from the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* (927), the *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, dated Jōgan 7 (865), records that the "Jōrin deity of Dewa Province" was promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In Gangyō 4 (880), the deity was further elevated to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade alongside deities such as {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, {{ill|Gassan Shrine|ja|月山神}}, and {{ill|Komonoimi Shrine|ja|小物忌神社}}.Edited by Tanigawa Kenichi, *The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 12: Tohoku & Hokkaido* (Hakusuisha, June 1984). The *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku* mentions Jōrin Shrine primarily after the 9th century, reflecting its prominence following the full establishment of Jōrin Castle. However, the shrine is absent from records after the medieval period, likely due to its decline during the Sengoku era. During the Edo period, the shrine was revitalized under the Sakai clan, lords of the Shōnai domain. In An'ei 4 (1775), a new shrine building was constructed. Later records indicate further repairs and contributions during the Hōei and Tenpō eras, as well as the rebuilding of the shrine after a fire in Meiji 6 (1873).Yamagata Prefecture Shrine Office, *Yamagata Prefecture Shrine History* (2000). In the modern era, the shrine underwent several reconstructions and administrative changes. It was designated as a Prefectural Shrine in Shōwa 7 (1932) and continues to host sacred rituals and cultural events, such as the *Mikomai* dances performed by local children. == deity == * [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] (倉稲魂命) == Auxiliary Shrines == * Tamaike Shrine * Kōtaijin Shrine == Access == * [[East Japan Railway Company|JR]] [[Uetsu Main Line]] [[Sakata Station]]: 20-minute bus ride * JR Uetsu Main Line [[Mototate Station]]: 30-minute walk == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Abe, Masami. ''Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho Vol. 5: Kinowa no Dewa Saku Ato Oyobi Kokubunji Ato Chōsa''. Kokusho Kankōkai, 1982. * Tanikawa, Kenichi (ed.). ''Nihon no Kamigami - Jinja to Seichi Vol. 12: Tōhoku/Hokkaidō''. Hakusuisha, 1984. * Yamagata Shrine Bureau. ''Yamagata Ken Jinja-shi''. Yamagata Shrine Bureau, 2000. {{commonscat|Kinowa-jinja}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kinowa Shrine}} [[Category:Kinowa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] While absent from the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* (927), the *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, dated Jōgan 7 (865), records that the "Jōrin deity of Dewa Province" was promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In Gangyō 4 (880), the deity was further elevated to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade alongside deities such as {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, {{ill|Gassan Shrine|ja|月山神}}, and {{ill|Komonoimi Shrine|ja|小物忌神社}}.Edited by Tanigawa Kenichi, *The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 12: Tohoku & Hokkaido* (Hakusuisha, June 1984). The *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku* mentions Jōrin Shrine primarily after the 9th century, reflecting its prominence following the full establishment of Jōrin Castle. However, the shrine is absent from records after the medieval period, likely due to its decline during the Sengoku era. During the Edo period, the shrine was revitalized under the Sakai clan, lords of the Shōnai domain. In An'ei 4 (1775), a new shrine building was constructed. Later records indicate further repairs and contributions during the Hōei and Tenpō eras, as well as the rebuilding of the shrine after a fire in Meiji 6 (1873).Yamagata Prefecture Shrine Office, *Yamagata Prefecture Shrine History* (2000). In the modern era, the shrine underwent several reconstructions and administrative changes. It was designated as a Prefectural Shrine in Shōwa 7 (1932) and continues to host sacred rituals and cultural events, such as the *Mikomai* dances performed by local children. == deity == * [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] (倉稲魂命) == Auxiliary Shrines == * Tamaike Shrine * Kōtaijin Shrine == Access == * [[East Japan Railway Company|JR]] [[Uetsu Main Line]] [[Sakata Station]]: 20-minute bus ride * JR Uetsu Main Line [[Mototate Station]]: 30-minute walk == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Abe, Masami. [[ja:城輪神社]] {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kinowa Shrine | image = [[File:Kinowa-jinja Haiden.JPG|250px|Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall]]
Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall | location = Kinowa, Omotomononimi 35, [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|38|57|59.00|N|139|54|36.50|E|scale:10000_region:JP-06_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinowa Shrine}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] | shrine_rank = [[Shakaku|Nationally recognized]]; [[Dewa Province]]'s [[Ichinomiya#Ninomiya|Ninomiya]]
[[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]] | founded = Unknown | honden = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festivals = August 17 (festival) | rituals = Mikashira-shinji (Sacred Head Ceremony) | map = {{location map|Japan Yamagata|lat_deg=38|lat_min=57|lat_sec=59.00|lon_deg=139|lon_min=54|lon_sec=36.50|label=Kinowa Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} [[File:Kinowa-jinja Gate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Torii Gate]] [[File:Kinowa-jinja Honden.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Main Hall]] [[File:Chokai with Kinowa-jinja.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine grounds. View from the northwest corner of the Kinowa Historical Park, located at the site of the ancient Kinowa Palisade. The mountain in the background is [[Mount Chōkai]].]] '''Kinowa Shrine''' (城輪神社, ''Kinowa-jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan. It was discovered in [[1931]] that the shrine is situated at the site of an ancient castle palisade, confirming its role as the guardian deity of the fortification. It is the [[Ninomiya]] of the [[Dewa Province]] and formerly held the rank of [[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]]. Although it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'', it appears in the ''[[Six National Histories|Rikkokushi]]'' as a [[Shakaku|Nationally Recognized Shrine]]. == History == The exact date of founding is unclear, but according to *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*Abe Masami, *Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho, Vol. 5: Jōrin no Dewasaku-shi Oyobi Kokubun-ji-shi Chōsa* (National Publishing Co., November 1982)., it is presumed that the shrine was established sometime between the construction of {{ill|Jōrin Castle|ja|城輪柵}} in the first year of the Wadō era (708) and the second year of the Reiki era (716). According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, a historical record written by the shrine's priest in the 4th year of the An'ei era (1775), the shrine was enshrined during the reign of Emperor Keikō. However, *Ihitsu no Suji*, written in Ansei 6 (1859), dismisses this claim as doubtful. In May of Shōwa 6 (1931), the ruins of Jōrin Castle were discovered, revealing that the shrine was located between the north gate and northeast corner of the castle's northern perimeter. The *Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple* states that the enshrinement along the northern perimeter gave the deity its name, Jōrin no Kami.*Ihitsu no Suji* (1859) proposes an alternate theory, suggesting that the name "Jōrin" derives from combining the kanji for "Jō" from Yamato Province's {{ill|Jōjō District|ja|城上郡}} and "Rin" from {{ill|Mount Miwa|ja|三輪山}}. It further hypothesizes that this deity was the tutelary protector of the Dewasaku, established to pray for the pacification and subjugation of the Emishi in the northeast, a region fortified heavily due to frequent attacks. Place names near the shrine, such as Miyagata, Tamada, Terayashiki, and Kamiyuda, became more meaningful after the discovery of Jōrin Castle ruins. Particularly, *Ninomiya Kokonki* notes that Miyagata referred to lands cultivated by shrine farmers, while Kōdengata referred to fields owned by the provincial government. According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, the enshrined deity of Jōrin Daimyōjin is Kuraokami-no-Mikoto, said to be identical to the deity of {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}. However, this claim is dismissed as speculative in *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*. While absent from the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* (927), the *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, dated Jōgan 7 (865), records that the "Jōrin deity of Dewa Province" was promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In Gangyō 4 (880), the deity was further elevated to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade alongside deities such as {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, {{ill|Gassan Shrine|ja|月山神}}, and {{ill|Komonoimi Shrine|ja|小物忌神社}}.Edited by Tanigawa Kenichi, *The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 12: Tohoku & Hokkaido* (Hakusuisha, June 1984). The *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku* mentions Jōrin Shrine primarily after the 9th century, reflecting its prominence following the full establishment of Jōrin Castle. However, the shrine is absent from records after the medieval period, likely due to its decline during the Sengoku era. During the Edo period, the shrine was revitalized under the Sakai clan, lords of the Shōnai domain. In An'ei 4 (1775), a new shrine building was constructed. Later records indicate further repairs and contributions during the Hōei and Tenpō eras, as well as the rebuilding of the shrine after a fire in Meiji 6 (1873).Yamagata Prefecture Shrine Office, *Yamagata Prefecture Shrine History* (2000). In the modern era, the shrine underwent several reconstructions and administrative changes. It was designated as a Prefectural Shrine in Shōwa 7 (1932) and continues to host sacred rituals and cultural events, such as the *Mikomai* dances performed by local children. == deity == * [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] (倉稲魂命) == Auxiliary Shrines == * Tamaike Shrine * Kōtaijin Shrine == Access == * [[East Japan Railway Company|JR]] [[Uetsu Main Line]] [[Sakata Station]]: 20-minute bus ride * JR Uetsu Main Line [[Mototate Station]]: 30-minute walk == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Abe, Masami. ''Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho Vol. 5: Kinowa no Dewa Saku Ato Oyobi Kokubunji Ato Chōsa''. Kokusho Kankōkai, 1982. * Tanikawa, Kenichi (ed.). ''Nihon no Kamigami - Jinja to Seichi Vol. 12: Tōhoku/Hokkaidō''. Hakusuisha, 1984. * Yamagata Shrine Bureau. ''Yamagata Ken Jinja-shi''. [[ja:城輪神社]] {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kinowa Shrine | image = [[File:Kinowa-jinja Haiden.JPG|250px|Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall]]
Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall | location = Kinowa, Omotomononimi 35, [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|38|57|59.00|N|139|54|36.50|E|scale:10000_region:JP-06_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinowa Shrine}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] | shrine_rank = [[Shakaku|Nationally recognized]]; [[Dewa Province]]'s [[Ichinomiya#Ninomiya|Ninomiya]]
[[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]] | founded = Unknown | honden = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festivals = August 17 (festival) | rituals = Mikashira-shinji (Sacred Head Ceremony) | map = {{location map|Japan Yamagata|lat_deg=38|lat_min=57|lat_sec=59.00|lon_deg=139|lon_min=54|lon_sec=36.50|label=Kinowa Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} [[File:Kinowa-jinja Gate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Torii Gate]] [[File:Kinowa-jinja Honden.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Main Hall]] [[File:Chokai with Kinowa-jinja.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine grounds. View from the northwest corner of the Kinowa Historical Park, located at the site of the ancient Kinowa Palisade. The mountain in the background is [[Mount Chōkai]].]] '''Kinowa Shrine''' (城輪神社, ''Kinowa-jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan. It was discovered in [[1931]] that the shrine is situated at the site of an ancient castle palisade, confirming its role as the guardian deity of the fortification. It is the [[Ninomiya]] of the [[Dewa Province]] and formerly held the rank of [[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]]. Although it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'', it appears in the ''[[Six National Histories|Rikkokushi]]'' as a [[Shakaku|Nationally Recognized Shrine]]. == History == The exact date of founding is unclear, but according to *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*Abe Masami, *Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho, Vol. 5: Jōrin no Dewasaku-shi Oyobi Kokubun-ji-shi Chōsa* (National Publishing Co., November 1982)., it is presumed that the shrine was established sometime between the construction of {{ill|Jōrin Castle|ja|城輪柵}} in the first year of the Wadō era (708) and the second year of the Reiki era (716). According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, a historical record written by the shrine's priest in the 4th year of the An'ei era (1775), the shrine was enshrined during the reign of Emperor Keikō. However, *Ihitsu no Suji*, written in Ansei 6 (1859), dismisses this claim as doubtful. In May of Shōwa 6 (1931), the ruins of Jōrin Castle were discovered, revealing that the shrine was located between the north gate and northeast corner of the castle's northern perimeter. The *Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple* states that the enshrinement along the northern perimeter gave the deity its name, Jōrin no Kami.*Ihitsu no Suji* (1859) proposes an alternate theory, suggesting that the name "Jōrin" derives from combining the kanji for "Jō" from Yamato Province's {{ill|Jōjō District|ja|城上郡}} and "Rin" from {{ill|Mount Miwa|ja|三輪山}}. It further hypothesizes that this deity was the tutelary protector of the Dewasaku, established to pray for the pacification and subjugation of the Emishi in the northeast, a region fortified heavily due to frequent attacks. Place names near the shrine, such as Miyagata, Tamada, Terayashiki, and Kamiyuda, became more meaningful after the discovery of Jōrin Castle ruins. Particularly, *Ninomiya Kokonki* notes that Miyagata referred to lands cultivated by shrine farmers, while Kōdengata referred to fields owned by the provincial government. According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, the enshrined deity of Jōrin Daimyōjin is Kuraokami-no-Mikoto, said to be identical to the deity of {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}. However, this claim is dismissed as speculative in *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*. While absent from the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* (927), the *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, dated Jōgan 7 (865), records that the "Jōrin deity of Dewa Province" was promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In Gangyō 4 (880), the deity was further elevated to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade alongside deities such as {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, {{ill|Gassan Shrine|ja|月山神}}, and {{ill|Komonoimi Shrine|ja|小物忌神社}}.Edited by Tanigawa Kenichi, *The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 12: Tohoku & Hokkaido* (Hakusuisha, June 1984). The *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku* mentions Jōrin Shrine primarily after the 9th century, reflecting its prominence following the full establishment of Jōrin Castle. However, the shrine is absent from records after the medieval period, likely due to its decline during the Sengoku era. During the Edo period, the shrine was revitalized under the Sakai clan, lords of the Shōnai domain. In An'ei 4 (1775), a new shrine building was constructed. Later records indicate further repairs and contributions during the Hōei and Tenpō eras, as well as the rebuilding of the shrine after a fire in Meiji 6 (1873).Yamagata Prefecture Shrine Office, *Yamagata Prefecture Shrine History* (2000). In the modern era, the shrine underwent several reconstructions and administrative changes. It was designated as a Prefectural Shrine in Shōwa 7 (1932) and continues to host sacred rituals and cultural events, such as the *Mikomai* dances performed by local children. == deity == * [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] (倉稲魂命) == Auxiliary Shrines == * Tamaike Shrine * Kōtaijin Shrine == Access == * [[East Japan Railway Company|JR]] [[Uetsu Main Line]] [[Sakata Station]]: 20-minute bus ride * JR Uetsu Main Line [[Mototate Station]]: 30-minute walk == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Abe, Masami. ''Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho Vol. 5: Kinowa no Dewa Saku Ato Oyobi Kokubunji Ato Chōsa''. Kokusho Kankōkai, 1982. * Tanikawa, Kenichi (ed.). ''Nihon no Kamigami - Jinja to Seichi Vol. 12: Tōhoku/Hokkaidō''. Hakusuisha, 1984. * Yamagata Shrine Bureau. ''Yamagata Ken Jinja-shi''. Yamagata Shrine Bureau, 2000. {{commonscat|Kinowa-jinja}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kinowa Shrine}} [[Category:Kinowa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq Particularly, *Ninomiya Kokonki* notes that Miyagata referred to lands cultivated by shrine farmers, while Kōdengata referred to fields owned by the provincial government. According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, the enshrined deity of Jōrin Daimyōjin is Kuraokami-no-Mikoto, said to be identical to the deity of {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}. However, this claim is dismissed as speculative in *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*. While absent from the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* (927), the *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, dated Jōgan 7 (865), records that the "Jōrin deity of Dewa Province" was promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In Gangyō 4 (880), the deity was further elevated to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade alongside deities such as {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, {{ill|Gassan Shrine|ja|月山神}}, and {{ill|Komonoimi Shrine|ja|小物忌神社}}.Edited by Tanigawa Kenichi, *The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 12: Tohoku & Hokkaido* (Hakusuisha, June 1984). The *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku* mentions Jōrin Shrine primarily after the 9th century, reflecting its prominence following the full establishment of Jōrin Castle. However, the shrine is absent from records after the medieval period, likely due to its decline during the Sengoku era. During the Edo period, the shrine was revitalized under the Sakai clan, lords of the Shōnai domain. In An'ei 4 (1775), a new shrine building was constructed. Later records indicate further repairs and contributions during the Hōei and Tenpō eras, as well as the rebuilding of the shrine after a fire in Meiji 6 (1873).Yamagata Prefecture Shrine Office, *Yamagata Prefecture Shrine History* (2000). In the modern era, the shrine underwent several reconstructions and administrative changes. It was designated as a Prefectural Shrine in Shōwa 7 (1932) and continues to host sacred rituals and cultural events, such as the *Mikomai* dances performed by local children. == deity == * [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] (倉稲魂命) == Auxiliary Shrines == * Tamaike Shrine * Kōtaijin Shrine == Access == * [[East Japan Railway Company|JR]] [[Uetsu Main Line]] [[Sakata Station]]: 20-minute bus ride * JR Uetsu Main Line [[Mototate Station]]: 30-minute walk == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Abe, Masami. ''Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho Vol. 5: Kinowa no Dewa Saku Ato Oyobi Kokubunji Ato Chōsa''. Kokusho Kankōkai, 1982. * Tanikawa, Kenichi (ed.). ''Nihon no Kamigami - Jinja to Seichi Vol. 12: Tōhoku/Hokkaidō''. Hakusuisha, 1984. * Yamagata Shrine Bureau. ''Yamagata Ken Jinja-shi''. [[ja:城輪神社]] {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kinowa Shrine | image = [[File:Kinowa-jinja Haiden.JPG|250px|Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall]]
Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall | location = Kinowa, Omotomononimi 35, [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|38|57|59.00|N|139|54|36.50|E|scale:10000_region:JP-06_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinowa Shrine}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] | shrine_rank = [[Shakaku|Nationally recognized]]; [[Dewa Province]]'s [[Ichinomiya#Ninomiya|Ninomiya]]
[[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]] | founded = Unknown | honden = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festivals = August 17 (festival) | rituals = Mikashira-shinji (Sacred Head Ceremony) | map = {{location map|Japan Yamagata|lat_deg=38|lat_min=57|lat_sec=59.00|lon_deg=139|lon_min=54|lon_sec=36.50|label=Kinowa Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} [[File:Kinowa-jinja Gate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Torii Gate]] [[File:Kinowa-jinja Honden.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Main Hall]] [[File:Chokai with Kinowa-jinja.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine grounds. View from the northwest corner of the Kinowa Historical Park, located at the site of the ancient Kinowa Palisade. The mountain in the background is [[Mount Chōkai]].]] '''Kinowa Shrine''' (城輪神社, ''Kinowa-jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan. It was discovered in [[1931]] that the shrine is situated at the site of an ancient castle palisade, confirming its role as the guardian deity of the fortification. It is the [[Ninomiya]] of the [[Dewa Province]] and formerly held the rank of [[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]]. Although it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'', it appears in the ''[[Six National Histories|Rikkokushi]]'' as a [[Shakaku|Nationally Recognized Shrine]]. == History == The exact date of founding is unclear, but according to *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*Abe Masami, *Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho, Vol. 5: Jōrin no Dewasaku-shi Oyobi Kokubun-ji-shi Chōsa* (National Publishing Co., November 1982)., it is presumed that the shrine was established sometime between the construction of {{ill|Jōrin Castle|ja|城輪柵}} in the first year of the Wadō era (708) and the second year of the Reiki era (716). According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, a historical record written by the shrine's priest in the 4th year of the An'ei era (1775), the shrine was enshrined during the reign of Emperor Keikō. However, *Ihitsu no Suji*, written in Ansei 6 (1859), dismisses this claim as doubtful. In May of Shōwa 6 (1931), the ruins of Jōrin Castle were discovered, revealing that the shrine was located between the north gate and northeast corner of the castle's northern perimeter. The *Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple* states that the enshrinement along the northern perimeter gave the deity its name, Jōrin no Kami.*Ihitsu no Suji* (1859) proposes an alternate theory, suggesting that the name "Jōrin" derives from combining the kanji for "Jō" from Yamato Province's {{ill|Jōjō District|ja|城上郡}} and "Rin" from {{ill|Mount Miwa|ja|三輪山}}. It further hypothesizes that this deity was the tutelary protector of the Dewasaku, established to pray for the pacification and subjugation of the Emishi in the northeast, a region fortified heavily due to frequent attacks. Place names near the shrine, such as Miyagata, Tamada, Terayashiki, and Kamiyuda, became more meaningful after the discovery of Jōrin Castle ruins. Particularly, *Ninomiya Kokonki* notes that Miyagata referred to lands cultivated by shrine farmers, while Kōdengata referred to fields owned by the provincial government. According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, the enshrined deity of Jōrin Daimyōjin is Kuraokami-no-Mikoto, said to be identical to the deity of {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}. However, this claim is dismissed as speculative in *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*. While absent from the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* (927), the *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, dated Jōgan 7 (865), records that the "Jōrin deity of Dewa Province" was promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In Gangyō 4 (880), the deity was further elevated to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade alongside deities such as {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, {{ill|Gassan Shrine|ja|月山神}}, and {{ill|Komonoimi Shrine|ja|小物忌神社}}.Edited by Tanigawa Kenichi, *The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 12: Tohoku & Hokkaido* (Hakusuisha, June 1984). The *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku* mentions Jōrin Shrine primarily after the 9th century, reflecting its prominence following the full establishment of Jōrin Castle. However, the shrine is absent from records after the medieval period, likely due to its decline during the Sengoku era. During the Edo period, the shrine was revitalized under the Sakai clan, lords of the Shōnai domain. In An'ei 4 (1775), a new shrine building was constructed. Later records indicate further repairs and contributions during the Hōei and Tenpō eras, as well as the rebuilding of the shrine after a fire in Meiji 6 (1873).Yamagata Prefecture Shrine Office, *Yamagata Prefecture Shrine History* (2000). In the modern era, the shrine underwent several reconstructions and administrative changes. It was designated as a Prefectural Shrine in Shōwa 7 (1932) and continues to host sacred rituals and cultural events, such as the *Mikomai* dances performed by local children. == deity == * [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] (倉稲魂命) == Auxiliary Shrines == * Tamaike Shrine * Kōtaijin Shrine == Access == * [[East Japan Railway Company|JR]] [[Uetsu Main Line]] [[Sakata Station]]: 20-minute bus ride * JR Uetsu Main Line [[Mototate Station]]: 30-minute walk == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Abe, Masami. ''Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho Vol. 5: Kinowa no Dewa Saku Ato Oyobi Kokubunji Ato Chōsa''. Kokusho Kankōkai, 1982. * Tanikawa, Kenichi (ed.). ''Nihon no Kamigami - Jinja to Seichi Vol. 12: Tōhoku/Hokkaidō''. Hakusuisha, 1984. * Yamagata Shrine Bureau. ''Yamagata Ken Jinja-shi''. Yamagata Shrine Bureau, 2000. {{commonscat|Kinowa-jinja}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{citation needed|Kinowa Shrine}} [[Category:Kinowa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:城輪神社]] {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kinowa Shrine | image = [[File:Kinowa-jinja Haiden.JPG|250px|Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall]]
Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall | location = Kinowa, Omotomononimi 35, [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|38|57|59.00|N|139|54|36.50|E|scale:10000_region:JP-06_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinowa Shrine}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] | shrine_rank = [[Shakaku|Nationally recognized]]; [[Dewa Province]]'s [[Ichinomiya#Ninomiya|Ninomiya]]
[[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]] | founded = Unknown | honden = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festivals = August 17 (festival) | rituals = Mikashira-shinji (Sacred Head Ceremony) | map = {{location map|Japan Yamagata|lat_deg=38|lat_min=57|lat_sec=59.00|lon_deg=139|lon_min=54|lon_sec=36.50|label=Kinowa Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} [[File:Kinowa-jinja Gate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Torii Gate]] [[File:Kinowa-jinja Honden.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Main Hall]] [[File:Chokai with Kinowa-jinja.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine grounds. View from the northwest corner of the Kinowa Historical Park, located at the site of the ancient Kinowa Palisade. The mountain in the background is [[Mount Chōkai]].]] '''Kinowa Shrine''' (城輪神社, ''Kinowa-jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan. It was discovered in [[1931]] that the shrine is situated at the site of an ancient castle palisade, confirming its role as the guardian deity of the fortification. It is the [[Ninomiya]] of the [[Dewa Province]] and formerly held the rank of [[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]]. Although it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'', it appears in the ''[[Six National Histories|Rikkokushi]]'' as a [[Shakaku|Nationally Recognized Shrine]]. == History == The exact date of founding is unclear, but according to *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*Abe Masami, *Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho, Vol. 5: Jōrin no Dewasaku-shi Oyobi Kokubun-ji-shi Chōsa* (National Publishing Co., November 1982)., it is presumed that the shrine was established sometime between the construction of {{ill|Jōrin Castle|ja|城輪柵}} in the first year of the Wadō era (708) and the second year of the Reiki era (716). According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, a historical record written by the shrine's priest in the 4th year of the An'ei era (1775), the shrine was enshrined during the reign of Emperor Keikō. However, *Ihitsu no Suji*, written in Ansei 6 (1859), dismisses this claim as doubtful. In May of Shōwa 6 (1931), the ruins of Jōrin Castle were discovered, revealing that the shrine was located between the north gate and northeast corner of the castle's northern perimeter. The *Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple* states that the enshrinement along the northern perimeter gave the deity its name, Jōrin no Kami.*Ihitsu no Suji* (1859) proposes an alternate theory, suggesting that the name "Jōrin" derives from combining the kanji for "Jō" from Yamato Province's {{ill|Jōjō District|ja|城上郡}} and "Rin" from {{ill|Mount Miwa|ja|三輪山}}. It further hypothesizes that this deity was the tutelary protector of the Dewasaku, established to pray for the pacification and subjugation of the Emishi in the northeast, a region fortified heavily due to frequent attacks. Place names near the shrine, such as Miyagata, Tamada, Terayashiki, and Kamiyuda, became more meaningful after the discovery of Jōrin Castle ruins. Particularly, *Ninomiya Kokonki* notes that Miyagata referred to lands cultivated by shrine farmers, while Kōdengata referred to fields owned by the provincial government. According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, the enshrined deity of Jōrin Daimyōjin is Kuraokami-no-Mikoto, said to be identical to the deity of {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}. However, this claim is dismissed as speculative in *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*. While absent from the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* (927), the *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, dated Jōgan 7 (865), records that the "Jōrin deity of Dewa Province" was promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In Gangyō 4 (880), the deity was further elevated to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade alongside deities such as {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, {{ill|Gassan Shrine|ja|月山神}}, and {{ill|Komonoimi Shrine|ja|小物忌神社}}.Edited by Tanigawa Kenichi, *The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 12: Tohoku & Hokkaido* (Hakusuisha, June 1984). The *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku* mentions Jōrin Shrine primarily after the 9th century, reflecting its prominence following the full establishment of Jōrin Castle. However, the shrine is absent from records after the medieval period, likely due to its decline during the Sengoku era. During the Edo period, the shrine was revitalized under the Sakai clan, lords of the Shōnai domain. In An'ei 4 (1775), a new shrine building was constructed. Later records indicate further repairs and contributions during the Hōei and Tenpō eras, as well as the rebuilding of the shrine after a fire in Meiji 6 (1873).Yamagata Prefecture Shrine Office, *Yamagata Prefecture Shrine History* (2000). In the modern era, the shrine underwent several reconstructions and administrative changes. It was designated as a Prefectural Shrine in Shōwa 7 (1932) and continues to host sacred rituals and cultural events, such as the *Mikomai* dances performed by local children. == deity == * [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] (倉稲魂命) == Auxiliary Shrines == * Tamaike Shrine * Kōtaijin Shrine == Access == * [[East Japan Railway Company|JR]] [[Uetsu Main Line]] [[Sakata Station]]: 20-minute bus ride * JR Uetsu Main Line [[Mototate Station]]: 30-minute walk == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Abe, Masami. ''Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho Vol. 5: Kinowa no Dewa Saku Ato Oyobi Kokubunji Ato Chōsa''. Kokusho Kankōkai, 1982. * Tanikawa, Kenichi (ed.). ''Nihon no Kamigami - Jinja to Seichi Vol. 12: Tōhoku/Hokkaidō''. Hakusuisha, 1984. * Yamagata Shrine Bureau. ''Yamagata Ken Jinja-shi''. Yamagata Shrine Bureau, 2000. {{commonscat|Kinowa-jinja}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{citation needed|Kinowa Shrine}} [[Category:Kinowa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:城輪神社]] {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kinowa Shrine | image = [[File:Kinowa-jinja Haiden.JPG|250px|Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall]]
Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall | location = Kinowa, Omotomononimi 35, [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|38|57|59.00|N|139|54|36.50|E|scale:10000_region:JP-06_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinowa Shrine}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] | shrine_rank = [[Shakaku|Nationally recognized]]; [[Dewa Province]]'s [[Ichinomiya#Ninomiya|Ninomiya]]
[[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]] | founded = Unknown | honden = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festivals = August 17 (festival) | rituals = Mikashira-shinji (Sacred Head Ceremony) | map = {{location map|Japan Yamagata|lat_deg=38|lat_min=57|lat_sec=59.00|lon_deg=139|lon_min=54|lon_sec=36.50|label=Kinowa Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} [[File:Kinowa-jinja Gate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Torii Gate]] [[File:Kinowa-jinja Honden.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Main Hall]] [[File:Chokai with Kinowa-jinja.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine grounds. View from the northwest corner of the Kinowa Historical Park, located at the site of the ancient Kinowa Palisade. The mountain in the background is [[Mount Chōkai]].]] '''Kinowa Shrine''' (城輪神社, ''Kinowa-jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan. It was discovered in [[1931]] that the shrine is situated at the site of an ancient castle palisade, confirming its role as the guardian deity of the fortification. It is the [[Ninomiya]] of the [[Dewa Province]] and formerly held the rank of [[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]]. Although it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'', it appears in the ''[[Six National Histories|Rikkokushi]]'' as a [[Shakaku|Nationally Recognized Shrine]]. == History == The exact date of founding is unclear, but according to *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*Abe Masami, *Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho, Vol. 5: Jōrin no Dewasaku-shi Oyobi Kokubun-ji-shi Chōsa* (National Publishing Co., November 1982)., it is presumed that the shrine was established sometime between the construction of {{ill|Jōrin Castle|ja|城輪柵}} in the first year of the Wadō era (708) and the second year of the Reiki era (716). According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, a historical record written by the shrine's priest in the 4th year of the An'ei era (1775), the shrine was enshrined during the reign of Emperor Keikō. However, *Ihitsu no Suji*, written in Ansei 6 (1859), dismisses this claim as doubtful. In May of Shōwa 6 (1931), the ruins of Jōrin Castle were discovered, revealing that the shrine was located between the north gate and northeast corner of the castle's northern perimeter. The *Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple* states that the enshrinement along the northern perimeter gave the deity its name, Jōrin no Kami.*Ihitsu no Suji* (1859) proposes an alternate theory, suggesting that the name "Jōrin" derives from combining the kanji for "Jō" from Yamato Province's {{ill|Jōjō District|ja|城上郡}} and "Rin" from {{ill|Mount Miwa|ja|三輪山}}. It further hypothesizes that this deity was the tutelary protector of the Dewasaku, established to pray for the pacification and subjugation of the Emishi in the northeast, a region fortified heavily due to frequent attacks. Place names near the shrine, such as Miyagata, Tamada, Terayashiki, and Kamiyuda, became more meaningful after the discovery of Jōrin Castle ruins. Particularly, *Ninomiya Kokonki* notes that Miyagata referred to lands cultivated by shrine farmers, while Kōdengata referred to fields owned by the provincial government. According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, the enshrined deity of Jōrin Daimyōjin is Kuraokami-no-Mikoto, said to be identical to the deity of {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}. However, this claim is dismissed as speculative in *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*. While absent from the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* (927), the *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, dated Jōgan 7 (865), records that the "Jōrin deity of Dewa Province" was promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In Gangyō 4 (880), the deity was further elevated to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade alongside deities such as {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, {{ill|Gassan Shrine|ja|月山神}}, and {{ill|Komonoimi Shrine|ja|小物忌神社}}.Edited by Tanigawa Kenichi, *The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 12: Tohoku & Hokkaido* (Hakusuisha, June 1984). The *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku* mentions Jōrin Shrine primarily after the 9th century, reflecting its prominence following the full establishment of Jōrin Castle. However, the shrine is absent from records after the medieval period, likely due to its decline during the Sengoku era. During the Edo period, the shrine was revitalized under the Sakai clan, lords of the Shōnai domain. In An'ei 4 (1775), a new shrine building was constructed. Later records indicate further repairs and contributions during the Hōei and Tenpō eras, as well as the rebuilding of the shrine after a fire in Meiji 6 (1873).Yamagata Prefecture Shrine Office, *Yamagata Prefecture Shrine History* (2000). In the modern era, the shrine underwent several reconstructions and administrative changes. It was designated as a Prefectural Shrine in Shōwa 7 (1932) and continues to host sacred rituals and cultural events, such as the *Mikomai* dances performed by local children. == deity == * [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] (倉稲魂命) == Auxiliary Shrines == * Tamaike Shrine * Kōtaijin Shrine == Access == * [[East Japan Railway Company|JR]] [[Uetsu Main Line]] [[Sakata Station]]: 20-minute bus ride * JR Uetsu Main Line [[Mototate Station]]: 30-minute walk == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Abe, Masami. ''Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho Vol. 5: Kinowa no Dewa Saku Ato Oyobi Kokubunji Ato Chōsa''. Kokusho Kankōkai, 1982. * Tanikawa, Kenichi (ed.). ''Nihon no Kamigami - Jinja to Seichi Vol. 12: Tōhoku/Hokkaidō''. Hakusuisha, 1984. * Yamagata Shrine Bureau. ''Yamagata Ken Jinja-shi''. Yamagata Shrine Bureau, 2000. {{commonscat|Kinowa-jinja}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{citation needed|Kinowa Shrine}} [[Category:Kinowa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] revision.text:
[[ja:城輪神社]] {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kinowa Shrine | image = [[File:Kinowa-jinja Haiden.JPG|250px|Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall]]
Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall | location = Kinowa, Omotomononimi 35, [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|38|57|59.00|N|139|54|36.50|E|scale:10000_region:JP-06_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinowa Shrine}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] | shrine_rank = [[Shakaku|Nationally recognized]]; [[Dewa Province]]'s [[Ichinomiya#Ninomiya|Ninomiya]]
[[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]] | founded = Unknown | honden = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festivals = August 17 (festival) | rituals = Mikashira-shinji (Sacred Head Ceremony) | map = {{location map|Japan Yamagata|lat_deg=38|lat_min=57|lat_sec=59.00|lon_deg=139|lon_min=54|lon_sec=36.50|label=Kinowa Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} [[File:Kinowa-jinja Gate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Torii Gate]] [[File:Kinowa-jinja Honden.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Main Hall]] [[File:Chokai with Kinowa-jinja.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine grounds. View from the northwest corner of the Kinowa Historical Park, located at the site of the ancient Kinowa Palisade. The mountain in the background is [[Mount Chōkai]].]] '''Kinowa Shrine''' (城輪神社, ''Kinowa-jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan. It was discovered in [[1931]] that the shrine is situated at the site of an ancient castle palisade, confirming its role as the guardian deity of the fortification. It is the [[Ninomiya]] of the [[Dewa Province]] and formerly held the rank of [[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]]. Although it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'', it appears in the ''[[Six National Histories|Rikkokushi]]'' as a [[Shakaku|Nationally Recognized Shrine]]. == History == The exact date of founding is unclear, but according to *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*Abe Masami, *Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho, Vol. 5: Jōrin no Dewasaku-shi Oyobi Kokubun-ji-shi Chōsa* (National Publishing Co., November 1982)., it is presumed that the shrine was established sometime between the construction of {{ill|Jōrin Castle|ja|城輪柵}} in the first year of the Wadō era (708) and the second year of the Reiki era (716). According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, a historical record written by the shrine's priest in the 4th year of the An'ei era (1775), the shrine was enshrined during the reign of Emperor Keikō. However, *Ihitsu no Suji*, written in Ansei 6 (1859), dismisses this claim as doubtful. In May of Shōwa 6 (1931), the ruins of Jōrin Castle were discovered, revealing that the shrine was located between the north gate and northeast corner of the castle's northern perimeter. The *Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple* states that the enshrinement along the northern perimeter gave the deity its name, Jōrin no Kami.*Ihitsu no Suji* (1859) proposes an alternate theory, suggesting that the name "Jōrin" derives from combining the kanji for "Jō" from Yamato Province's {{ill|Jōjō District|ja|城上郡}} and "Rin" from {{ill|Mount Miwa|ja|三輪山}}. It further hypothesizes that this deity was the tutelary protector of the Dewasaku, established to pray for the pacification and subjugation of the Emishi in the northeast, a region fortified heavily due to frequent attacks. Place names near the shrine, such as Miyagata, Tamada, Terayashiki, and Kamiyuda, became more meaningful after the discovery of Jōrin Castle ruins. Particularly, *Ninomiya Kokonki* notes that Miyagata referred to lands cultivated by shrine farmers, while Kōdengata referred to fields owned by the provincial government. According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, the enshrined deity of Jōrin Daimyōjin is Kuraokami-no-Mikoto, said to be identical to the deity of {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}. However, this claim is dismissed as speculative in *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*. While absent from the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* (927), the *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, dated Jōgan 7 (865), records that the "Jōrin deity of Dewa Province" was promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In Gangyō 4 (880), the deity was further elevated to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade alongside deities such as {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, {{ill|Gassan Shrine|ja|月山神}}, and {{ill|Komonoimi Shrine|ja|小物忌神社}}.Edited by Tanigawa Kenichi, *The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 12: Tohoku & Hokkaido* (Hakusuisha, June 1984). The *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku* mentions Jōrin Shrine primarily after the 9th century, reflecting its prominence following the full establishment of Jōrin Castle. However, the shrine is absent from records after the medieval period, likely due to its decline during the Sengoku era. During the Edo period, the shrine was revitalized under the Sakai clan, lords of the Shōnai domain. In An'ei 4 (1775), a new shrine building was constructed. Later records indicate further repairs and contributions during the Hōei and Tenpō eras, as well as the rebuilding of the shrine after a fire in Meiji 6 (1873).Yamagata Prefecture Shrine Office, *Yamagata Prefecture Shrine History* (2000). In the modern era, the shrine underwent several reconstructions and administrative changes. It was designated as a Prefectural Shrine in Shōwa 7 (1932) and continues to host sacred rituals and cultural events, such as the *Mikomai* dances performed by local children. == deity == * [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] (倉稲魂命) == Auxiliary Shrines == * Tamaike Shrine * Kōtaijin Shrine == Access == * [[East Japan Railway Company|JR]] [[Uetsu Main Line]] [[Sakata Station]]: 20-minute bus ride * JR Uetsu Main Line [[Mototate Station]]: 30-minute walk == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Abe, Masami. ''Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho Vol. 5: Kinowa no Dewa Saku Ato Oyobi Kokubunji Ato Chōsa''. Kokusho Kankōkai, 1982. * Tanikawa, Kenichi (ed.). ''Nihon no Kamigami - Jinja to Seichi Vol. 12: Tōhoku/Hokkaidō''. Hakusuisha, 1984. * Yamagata Shrine Bureau. ''Yamagata Ken Jinja-shi''. Yamagata Shrine Bureau, 2000. {{commonscat|Kinowa-jinja}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{citation needed|Kinowa Shrine}} [[Category:Kinowa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:城輪神社]] {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kinowa Shrine | image = [[File:Kinowa-jinja Haiden.JPG|250px|Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall]]
Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall | location = Kinowa, Omotomononimi 35, [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|38|57|59.00|N|139|54|36.50|E|scale:10000_region:JP-06_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinowa Shrine}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] | shrine_rank = [[Shakaku|Nationally recognized]]; [[Dewa Province]]'s [[Ichinomiya#Ninomiya|Ninomiya]]
[[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]] | founded = Unknown | honden = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festivals = August 17 (festival) | rituals = Mikashira-shinji (Sacred Head Ceremony) | map = {{location map|Japan Yamagata|lat_deg=38|lat_min=57|lat_sec=59.00|lon_deg=139|lon_min=54|lon_sec=36.50|label=Kinowa Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} [[File:Kinowa-jinja Gate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Torii Gate]] [[File:Kinowa-jinja Honden.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Main Hall]] [[File:Chokai with Kinowa-jinja.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine grounds. View from the northwest corner of the Kinowa Historical Park, located at the site of the ancient Kinowa Palisade. The mountain in the background is [[Mount Chōkai]].]] '''Kinowa Shrine''' (城輪神社, ''Kinowa-jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan. It was discovered in [[1931]] that the shrine is situated at the site of an ancient castle palisade, confirming its role as the guardian deity of the fortification. It is the [[Ninomiya]] of the [[Dewa Province]] and formerly held the rank of [[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]]. Although it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'', it appears in the ''[[Six National Histories|Rikkokushi]]'' as a [[Shakaku|Nationally Recognized Shrine]]. == History == The exact date of founding is unclear, but according to *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*Abe Masami, *Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho, Vol. 5: Jōrin no Dewasaku-shi Oyobi Kokubun-ji-shi Chōsa* (National Publishing Co., November 1982)., it is presumed that the shrine was established sometime between the construction of {{ill|Jōrin Castle|ja|城輪柵}} in the first year of the Wadō era (708) and the second year of the Reiki era (716). According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, a historical record written by the shrine's priest in the 4th year of the An'ei era (1775), the shrine was enshrined during the reign of Emperor Keikō. However, *Ihitsu no Suji*, written in Ansei 6 (1859), dismisses this claim as doubtful. In May of Shōwa 6 (1931), the ruins of Jōrin Castle were discovered, revealing that the shrine was located between the north gate and northeast corner of the castle's northern perimeter. The *Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple* states that the enshrinement along the northern perimeter gave the deity its name, Jōrin no Kami.*Ihitsu no Suji* (1859) proposes an alternate theory, suggesting that the name "Jōrin" derives from combining the kanji for "Jō" from Yamato Province's {{ill|Jōjō District|ja|城上郡}} and "Rin" from {{ill|Mount Miwa|ja|三輪山}}. It further hypothesizes that this deity was the tutelary protector of the Dewasaku, established to pray for the pacification and subjugation of the Emishi in the northeast, a region fortified heavily due to frequent attacks. Place names near the shrine, such as Miyagata, Tamada, Terayashiki, and Kamiyuda, became more meaningful after the discovery of Jōrin Castle ruins. Particularly, *Ninomiya Kokonki* notes that Miyagata referred to lands cultivated by shrine farmers, while Kōdengata referred to fields owned by the provincial government. According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, the enshrined deity of Jōrin Daimyōjin is Kuraokami-no-Mikoto, said to be identical to the deity of {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}. However, this claim is dismissed as speculative in *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*. While absent from the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* (927), the *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, dated Jōgan 7 (865), records that the "Jōrin deity of Dewa Province" was promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In Gangyō 4 (880), the deity was further elevated to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade alongside deities such as {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, {{ill|Gassan Shrine|ja|月山神}}, and {{ill|Komonoimi Shrine|ja|小物忌神社}}.Edited by Tanigawa Kenichi, *The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 12: Tohoku & Hokkaido* (Hakusuisha, June 1984). The *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku* mentions Jōrin Shrine primarily after the 9th century, reflecting its prominence following the full establishment of Jōrin Castle. However, the shrine is absent from records after the medieval period, likely due to its decline during the Sengoku era. During the Edo period, the shrine was revitalized under the Sakai clan, lords of the Shōnai domain. In An'ei 4 (1775), a new shrine building was constructed. Later records indicate further repairs and contributions during the Hōei and Tenpō eras, as well as the rebuilding of the shrine after a fire in Meiji 6 (1873).Yamagata Prefecture Shrine Office, *Yamagata Prefecture Shrine History* (2000). In the modern era, the shrine underwent several reconstructions and administrative changes. It was designated as a Prefectural Shrine in Shōwa 7 (1932) and continues to host sacred rituals and cultural events, such as the *Mikomai* dances performed by local children. == deity == * [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] (倉稲魂命) == Auxiliary Shrines == * Tamaike Shrine * Kōtaijin Shrine == Access == * [[East Japan Railway Company|JR]] [[Uetsu Main Line]] [[Sakata Station]]: 20-minute bus ride * JR Uetsu Main Line [[Mototate Station]]: 30-minute walk == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Abe, Masami. revision.text:
[[ja:城輪神社]] {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kinowa Shrine | image = [[File:Kinowa-jinja Haiden.JPG|250px|Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall]]
Kinowa Shrine - Worship Hall | location = Kinowa, Omotomononimi 35, [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|38|57|59.00|N|139|54|36.50|E|scale:10000_region:JP-06_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinowa Shrine}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] | shrine_rank = [[Shakaku|Nationally recognized]]; [[Dewa Province]]'s [[Ichinomiya#Ninomiya|Ninomiya]]
[[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]] | founded = Unknown | honden = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festivals = August 17 (festival) | rituals = Mikashira-shinji (Sacred Head Ceremony) | map = {{location map|Japan Yamagata|lat_deg=38|lat_min=57|lat_sec=59.00|lon_deg=139|lon_min=54|lon_sec=36.50|label=Kinowa Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} [[File:Kinowa-jinja Gate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Torii Gate]] [[File:Kinowa-jinja Honden.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine - Main Hall]] [[File:Chokai with Kinowa-jinja.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kinowa Shrine grounds. View from the northwest corner of the Kinowa Historical Park, located at the site of the ancient Kinowa Palisade. The mountain in the background is [[Mount Chōkai]].]] '''Kinowa Shrine''' (城輪神社, ''Kinowa-jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sakata, Yamagata]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan. It was discovered in [[1931]] that the shrine is situated at the site of an ancient castle palisade, confirming its role as the guardian deity of the fortification. It is the [[Ninomiya]] of the [[Dewa Province]] and formerly held the rank of [[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]]. Although it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'', it appears in the ''[[Six National Histories|Rikkokushi]]'' as a [[Shakaku|Nationally Recognized Shrine]]. == History == The exact date of founding is unclear, but according to *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*Abe Masami, *Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho, Vol. 5: Jōrin no Dewasaku-shi Oyobi Kokubun-ji-shi Chōsa* (National Publishing Co., November 1982)., it is presumed that the shrine was established sometime between the construction of {{ill|Jōrin Castle|ja|城輪柵}} in the first year of the Wadō era (708) and the second year of the Reiki era (716). According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, a historical record written by the shrine's priest in the 4th year of the An'ei era (1775), the shrine was enshrined during the reign of Emperor Keikō. However, *Ihitsu no Suji*, written in Ansei 6 (1859), dismisses this claim as doubtful. In May of Shōwa 6 (1931), the ruins of Jōrin Castle were discovered, revealing that the shrine was located between the north gate and northeast corner of the castle's northern perimeter. The *Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple* states that the enshrinement along the northern perimeter gave the deity its name, Jōrin no Kami.*Ihitsu no Suji* (1859) proposes an alternate theory, suggesting that the name "Jōrin" derives from combining the kanji for "Jō" from Yamato Province's {{ill|Jōjō District|ja|城上郡}} and "Rin" from {{ill|Mount Miwa|ja|三輪山}}. It further hypothesizes that this deity was the tutelary protector of the Dewasaku, established to pray for the pacification and subjugation of the Emishi in the northeast, a region fortified heavily due to frequent attacks. Place names near the shrine, such as Miyagata, Tamada, Terayashiki, and Kamiyuda, became more meaningful after the discovery of Jōrin Castle ruins. Particularly, *Ninomiya Kokonki* notes that Miyagata referred to lands cultivated by shrine farmers, while Kōdengata referred to fields owned by the provincial government. According to *Ninomiya Kokonki*, the enshrined deity of Jōrin Daimyōjin is Kuraokami-no-Mikoto, said to be identical to the deity of {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}. However, this claim is dismissed as speculative in *The Investigation of the Ruins of the Dewasaku Castle and Kokubun-ji Temple*. While absent from the *Engishiki Jinmyōchō* (927), the *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, dated Jōgan 7 (865), records that the "Jōrin deity of Dewa Province" was promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. In Gangyō 4 (880), the deity was further elevated to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade alongside deities such as {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, {{ill|Gassan Shrine|ja|月山神}}, and {{ill|Komonoimi Shrine|ja|小物忌神社}}.Edited by Tanigawa Kenichi, *The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites, Volume 12: Tohoku & Hokkaido* (Hakusuisha, June 1984). The *Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku* mentions Jōrin Shrine primarily after the 9th century, reflecting its prominence following the full establishment of Jōrin Castle. However, the shrine is absent from records after the medieval period, likely due to its decline during the Sengoku era. During the Edo period, the shrine was revitalized under the Sakai clan, lords of the Shōnai domain. In An'ei 4 (1775), a new shrine building was constructed. Later records indicate further repairs and contributions during the Hōei and Tenpō eras, as well as the rebuilding of the shrine after a fire in Meiji 6 (1873).Yamagata Prefecture Shrine Office, *Yamagata Prefecture Shrine History* (2000). In the modern era, the shrine underwent several reconstructions and administrative changes. It was designated as a Prefectural Shrine in Shōwa 7 (1932) and continues to host sacred rituals and cultural events, such as the *Mikomai* dances performed by local children. == deity == * [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] (倉稲魂命) == Auxiliary Shrines == * Tamaike Shrine * Kōtaijin Shrine == Access == * [[East Japan Railway Company|JR]] [[Uetsu Main Line]] [[Sakata Station]]: 20-minute bus ride * JR Uetsu Main Line [[Mototate Station]]: 30-minute walk == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Abe, Masami. ''Yamagata Kyōdo Kenkyū Sōsho Vol. 5: Kinowa no Dewa Saku Ato Oyobi Kokubunji Ato Chōsa''. Kokusho Kankōkai, 1982. * Tanikawa, Kenichi (ed.). ''Nihon no Kamigami - Jinja to Seichi Vol. 12: Tōhoku/Hokkaidō''. Hakusuisha, 1984. * Yamagata Shrine Bureau. ''Yamagata Ken Jinja-shi''. Yamagata Shrine Bureau, 2000. {{commonscat|Kinowa-jinja}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{citation needed|Kinowa Shrine}} [[Category:Kinowa Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kinowa
